shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: the moon full over the lake, its cold lane laid across the water

segments.text: Of all the lights that cross the garden's roof, only one reaches down and touches the world. The sun warms it. The stars decorate it. But the moon — the moon moves it.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: night: the waterline advancing up the sand, silent and certain

segments.text: Watch the beach under a climbing moon, and you can see the connection with your own eyes: the light rises, and the water rises to meet it.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: from the bright disc above down its reflection to the swelling lake

segments.text: This is the story of the garden's strangest partnership. The moon, and the tides.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: the whole lake from above, its outline subtly but visibly breathing

segments.text: The mechanism is invisible, which makes it the best magic in the garden. The moon leans on the water — not with light, but with attraction itself — and the water, everywhere at once, answers. The lake does not flow anywhere. It swells, as if the whole drowned country had drawn a breath.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: macro at the waterline: water climbing blade by blade into the grass margin

segments.text: At the shore, that breath becomes territory. Nearly half a meter of rise at the full — enough to drown the low trails, flood the grass margins, and redraw the strictest border on the map. Then the pull relents, and the water walks back down the beach it just climbed.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: the tide line drawn on the sand: wet dark below, dry pale above

segments.text: Twice per lunar day, without exception, without effort. High water, low water, high water again — the garden's oldest schedule, kept by its most distant clock.

shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: following the moon's arc across the dome in accelerated night

segments.text: But listen longer, and the rhythm has a rhythm. Over many days, the tides themselves swell and shrink — some days the water surges far into the meadow; other days it barely fingers the sand.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: split mood: one enormous tide flooding the margins, then a timid one

segments.text: Sailors on our own world named these moods centuries ago: spring tides, when the pull runs strongest, and neap tides, when it slackens — the slow envelope wrapped around the fast pulse. The garden's sea keeps both times at once, a drummer with two hands.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: gentle overlapping ripples, pattern within pattern

segments.text: Nothing else here is so precisely musical. The wind is improvisation; the seasonsless days repeat plainly. Only the tide plays in polyrhythm — and it has never missed a beat.

shot.subject: fish
shot.notes: fish gliding over newly drowned meadow at high water

segments.text: For the residents, the schedule is simply weather that keeps its promises. The fish ride each flood into borrowed country and withdraw before the lease expires.

shot.subject: ant
shot.notes: ants working the freshly exposed wet zone at low water

segments.text: The land nations work the ebb: at low water, the exposed margin is new land — damp, rich, and briefly roadworthy, until the drummer's other hand takes it back.

shot.subject: ladybug
shot.notes: a ladybug hurrying, slightly too casually, off ground beginning to darken

segments.text: And a few citizens, as episode twenty-three will confirm, treat the schedule with insufficient respect. The tide keeps no grudges; it also grants no exceptions. Punctuality, at the shore, is a survival skill.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: from the sand, the returning waterline approaching the lens

segments.text: Nothing in the garden enforces its law so gently, or so absolutely.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: the wet band between high and low water: dark, glistening, printed with traffic

segments.text: Between the highest reach of the water and its lowest retreat lies the garden's newest country — the tideline. Twice owned, never settled, and richer than either nation that claims it. The wet band is printed daily with the traffic of both: fin-strokes in the drowned hours, ant roads in the dry ones.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: low water: ants working the exposed band while it lasts, moving quickly

segments.text: Watch the land nation work an ebb, and notice the tempo. The ants on the tideline move faster than the ants of the high meadow — the same animal, on a deadline. Nothing teaches urgency like ground that keeps a schedule. The wet band is the only place in the garden where the residents visibly know the time.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: high water: fish gleaning the same band from the other side, methodical

segments.text: Six of the water's hours later, the same ground works its other shift. The fish glean the flooded band with the same efficiency and the opposite passport — harvesting what the land traffic left, softened and salted by the flood. Two nations, one field, and a wall of time between them where a wall of space should be.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: the tideline at the turn: water slack, neither advancing nor retreating, both nations near

segments.text: And at slack water — the few still minutes when the moon's pull balances and the border rests — the two shifts stand nearest each other, separated by centimeters and by everything. Neither knows the other works its ground. The tide keeps them strangers, and keeps them fed, with the same motion.

shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: the moon high and small over the whole breathing world

segments.text: Consider what the moon actually is to this garden: a clock nobody winds, a landlord nobody sees, a power that touches every shoreline without once touching the shore.

shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: the pale disc alone in frame, silent

segments.text: The ants will never suspect it. The fish will never thank it. Even the Visitor, who might work it out, has beaches to walk. The moon does not mind. Attraction, at any scale, is patient.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: moonlight on the swelling lake, the beach drinking the advancing edge

segments.text: And below, on schedule, the water stands up again to follow its light around the world.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: clouds slide across the moon; the silver lane on the lake goes out

segments.text: But the sky does not always keep such gentle company. There are nights when the roof forgets its manners entirely — when the window closes, and the dome turns to noise and fire.

shot.subject: lightning
shot.notes: a bolt tears the dark; one violent frame of white

segments.text: Rain, snow, and the sky's own artillery. Next: the Weather.
